title: The 10 tools I actually use as a .NET developer in 2026
author: Anton Martyniuk
tldr: A .NET developer's honest, unsponsored 2026 stack — two AI coding agents, two IDEs, and the research, diagramming, voice, meeting, writing and slide tools that fill in the rest.
summary:
The developer toolkit looks very different in 2026 than it did even a year ago. AI is no longer a side feature I tap into occasionally — it sits at the center of how I write code, design systems, run meetings and learn. Here are the ten tools I actually use with success as a .NET developer, architect and tech lead. I'm not sponsored by any of them; if you adopt even a few, you'll feel the difference in your daily workflow almost immediately.

For AI coding I lean on two agents. **Claude Code** is my main one: it runs from the terminal or desktop, reads my files, makes changes, runs tests and reports back. What makes it stand out for .NET is Plan Mode (it drafts a step-by-step plan I approve before it touches code), the way it follows my `CLAUDE.md` rules (Vertical Slice structure, `Result` instead of thrown exceptions, primary constructors), Git worktrees so it can work on a separate branch in the background, built-in code reviews (`/code-review` locally or `/ultrareview` for a multi-agent cloud review), reusable Skills, connectors to GitHub/Jira/Slack, and Cowork for non-coding desk work. I reach for **Claude Opus 4.7** on the heavy lifting. **OpenAI Codex** is a strong second — a terminal agent plus a cloud agent in ChatGPT that clones your repo and opens a pull request — but for .NET I still prefer Claude Code's code quality.

My IDE is **JetBrains Rider**: the gold-standard refactoring engine in .NET, a built-in decompiler, smooth handling of 100-plus-project solutions, first-class frontend support, and the Junie AI agent that I like far more than Copilot in Visual Studio. **Cursor** is my pick when I want a VS Code base with AI injected into every part of the editor — especially in polyglot repos where C# sits next to Python, TypeScript or Terraform.

The rest of the stack covers everything around the code. **NotebookLM** turns my pile of RFCs, whitepapers and audits into a research assistant that answers with citations to the source passages. **Eraser.io** brings diagram-as-code, so architecture diagrams live in Git and update from text instead of dragging boxes around (Excalidraw is my quick-sketch bonus). **Wispr Flow** is system-wide voice-to-text that pushes my writing from roughly 80 to 150–200 words per minute. **Granola** captures clean meeting notes — summaries, decisions, action items — without joining the call as a bot. **Grammarly** quietly fixes my writing everywhere, which matters a lot when English isn't your first language. And **Gamma** generates a full slide deck from a prompt or an existing doc in under a minute.

You don't need all ten. I lay out four stacks by budget: a surprisingly capable **$0** free tier (Rider non-commercial, Cursor Hobby, NotebookLM, Granola, Grammarly, Gamma, Excalidraw); a **~$50** "smart starter" that puts the money into AI coding; a **~$100** "productive professional" set with two coding agents plus meetings, writing and slides; and a **~$500** tier that removes every limit for people shipping production code daily. My advice: start around $50, get familiar with the tools, and upgrade later where it matters most for your projects.
keyPoints: AI now sits at the center of my .NET workflow, and none of these ten picks are sponsored.
Claude Code is my primary AI coding agent (Plan Mode, CLAUDE.md rules, worktrees, code reviews, Skills, Cowork); OpenAI Codex is a strong second.
Rider is my .NET IDE of choice (refactoring, decompiler, Junie agent); Cursor wins for VS Code-based, polyglot work.
Around the code I rely on NotebookLM, Eraser.io, Wispr Flow, Granola, Grammarly and Gamma.
I map four budget stacks ($0, ~$50, ~$100, ~$500) and suggest starting near $50, then upgrading where it matters.

quiz.question: Which AI coding agent does the author prefer for .NET work?
quiz.explanation: He keeps Codex as a strong second option but prefers Claude Code's code quality for .NET.

quiz.question: What approach does Eraser.io take to architecture diagrams?
options: Diagram-as-code that lives in Git and updates from text
Automatic UML generated from compiled binaries
quiz.explanation: Eraser.io describes diagrams as text, so they version alongside code and update when the text changes.

quiz.question: Why does the author rate Wispr Flow?
options: It joins your meetings as a bot
It is a system-wide voice-to-text tool that roughly doubles writing speed
It generates slide decks from a prompt
quiz.explanation: Voice-to-text pushes writing from about 80 to 150–200 words per minute.

quiz.question: At the ~$50/month tier, where does the author say to put the budget?
quiz.explanation: The "smart starter" stack prioritizes AI coding above everything else.
